refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall maintain teleoperation link availability ≥ 0.999 (99.9%) over the agreed evaluation period, measured as the ratio of time the bidirectional command-and-telemetry link is operational to total evaluation time.
refined_fr.rationale: CR-NGHE-010 addresses a critical operational requirement: remote operators must reliably command and receive feedback from equipment. The 0.999 threshold (approximately 43 minutes downtime per month) is a standard for safety-critical teleoperation. Refinement clarifies measurement scope (bidirectional link), evaluation period (customer-defined), and the quantitative target.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Continuous monitoring of link state transitions (up/down) during the evaluation period; calculation of cumulative uptime / total period; comparison against 0.999 threshold. Requires instrumentation of Network Link state and timestamped logging.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-010.1: The teleoperation system shall establish and maintain a bidirectional command link from tele-operator to vehicle.
FR-NGHE-010.2: The teleoperation system shall establish and maintain a bidirectional telemetry link from vehicle to tele-operator.
FR-NGHE-010.3: The teleoperation system shall detect link failures and initiate recovery within defined time bounds.
FR-NGHE-010.4: The teleoperation system shall log link state events (up/down transitions) with timestamps for availability calculation.

candidate_dps: DP-A: Implement redundant network paths (primary + backup) with automatic failover; monitor link health continuously; log state transitions.
DP-B: Implement single primary network path with periodic health checks and manual recovery procedures.
DP-C: Implement multi-carrier network aggregation (e.g., LTE + satellite) with load balancing and seamless switchover; continuous monitoring and automated recovery.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP-C: Implement multi-carrier network aggregation (LTE + satellite or equivalent) with load balancing, seamless switchover, continuous link health monitoring, and automated recovery. Maintain real-time link state telemetry and event logging for availability calculation.

potential_sources: DP-C couples to network infrastructure availability (external dependency, not fully controllable by NGHE).
DP-C couples to tele-operator training and procedures (human factors in failover scenarios).
DP-C couples to vehicle power budget and antenna design (physical constraints).
coupling_check.notes: Couplings are manageable via interface contracts (Network Link interface specifies SLA), operator procedures, and design margins. Multi-carrier approach reduces single-point-of-failure risk inherent in DP-B. DP-A is intermediate but DP-C better addresses high safety criticality by eliminating manual recovery dependency.

assumptions_risks: Assumption: Multi-carrier network services (LTE + satellite) are available at the worksite.
Assumption: Vehicle can accommodate dual network hardware (antenna, modem, power).
Risk: Satellite latency may exceed operator tolerance; mitigation: design for LTE primary with satellite as backup.
Risk: Failover logic complexity increases software defect probability; mitigation: rigorous testing and formal verification of state machine.
Risk: Cost of dual-carrier hardware and service subscriptions may exceed budget; mitigation: early cost-benefit analysis with customer.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Clarify with customer the acceptable latency budget for each carrier (LTE vs. satellite) and the maximum failover time. Confirm worksite network coverage assumptions. Establish acceptance criteria for link recovery time (e.g., <5 seconds) as a sub-requirement.

im_boundary.name: Teleoperation
im_boundary.scope: The Teleoperation module owns the end-to-end teleoperation link availability logic, including multi-carrier network abstraction, failover control, link health monitoring, state machine, and event logging. It interfaces with the Network Link (physical/service layer) and provides Tasking (commands to vehicle) and Telemetry (feedback to tele-operator) to external consumers.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Bidirectional network service (LTE + satellite or equivalent). Teleoperation module consumes raw network connectivity and provides link health queries. Network Link reports carrier availability, latency, and packet loss metrics.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Commands from tele-operator (via Teleoperation module) to Vehicle. Teleoperation module encapsulates commands and routes them over the active carrier.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Sensor and state data from Vehicle to tele-operator (via Teleoperation module). Teleoperation module receives telemetry over the active carrier and forwards to tele-operator display.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Link failure or degradation alerts to Safety Services. Teleoperation module reports link state transitions and recovery events for safety-critical decision-making.

module_containment_scores.notes: Teleoperation is the natural owner of link availability logic, failover state machine, and monitoring. Minimal diffusion: only Network Link interface (external service) and SafetyAlert notification (outbound). High cohesion: all link management logic is contained.

Vehicle module owns the vehicle-side network hardware and antenna, but link availability logic (failover, monitoring, state machine) is not vehicle-specific; it is system-level. Assigning DP-C to Vehicle would scatter link management across vehicle and ground systems, violating cohesion.

Safety Services owns safety policy enforcement and alerts, but not the operational link management logic. Link availability is an operational requirement, not a safety enforcement function. Assigning DP-C to Safety Services would couple safety logic to network management, violating separation of concerns.
NGHE Solution is the system-level container, but assigning DP-C here would bypass architectural layering and prevent clear ownership. Teleoperation is a more specific and cohesive boundary.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: The Teleoperation module is the optimal owner because: (1) it is the functional domain for link management and failover logic; (2) it has clear external interfaces (Network Link, Tasking, Telemetry, SafetyAlert); (3) it minimizes diffusion of link state machine and monitoring logic; (4) it enables independent testing and verification of link availability; (5) it aligns with operational responsibility (tele-operator and teleoperation system are the primary stakeholders).
containment_risks: Risk: Network Link interface may not expose sufficient carrier-level metrics (latency, packet loss, signal strength) for intelligent failover decisions. Mitigation: establish detailed Network Link SLA and interface specification early.

Risk: Vehicle-side network hardware (antenna, modem) may have firmware or driver issues that degrade link quality. Mitigation: Teleoperation module must include diagnostics and logging to detect vehicle-side faults; escalate to Vehicle module for hardware remediation.
Risk: SafetyAlert interface may not be defined with sufficient detail (e.g., alert latency, acknowledgment protocol). Mitigation: coordinate with Safety Services to establish SafetyAlert contract.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: Establish a detailed interface specification for Network Link (carrier metrics, failover triggers, recovery procedures) and SafetyAlert (alert types, latency, acknowledgment). Conduct a design review with Vehicle and Safety Services modules to confirm interface contracts and identify any hidden couplings.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP-C (multi-carrier aggregation with seamless failover and monitoring) directly addresses FR-NGHE-010 (link availability ≥ 0.999). The DP specifies a concrete solution approach (redundancy, failover, monitoring, logging) that is traceable to the FR. No logical gaps or misalignments detected.

diffusion_risks: Diffusion risk: Network Link interface is external (not owned by Teleoperation); Teleoperation depends on Network Link SLA. Mitigation: establish and enforce Network Link interface contract.

Diffusion risk: Vehicle-side network hardware faults may degrade link quality; Teleoperation must detect and report these faults. Mitigation: include vehicle-side diagnostics in Teleoperation module or establish a diagnostic interface with Vehicle module.
Diffusion risk: SafetyAlert notification may introduce latency or coupling to Safety Services state machine. Mitigation: design SafetyAlert as a fire-and-forget event stream to minimize coupling.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Teleoperation module contains the link availability logic (failover state machine, monitoring, logging) with high cohesion. External dependencies (Network Link, SafetyAlert) are well-defined interfaces. No significant diffusion of link management logic across other modules. Containment score 0.95 is justified.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: Verification is feasible: (1) Instrument Network Link interface to log carrier state transitions and metrics. (2) Implement link state logging in Teleoperation module. (3) Run evaluation period (customer-defined, e.g., 30 days) and calculate uptime / total time. (4) Compare against 0.999 threshold. (5) Conduct failure mode testing (simulate carrier outages, latency spikes) to validate failover logic.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Allocation is clear: Teleoperation module is responsible for achieving link availability ≥ 0.999. Success criteria: measured availability ≥ 0.999 over evaluation period. Failure criteria: measured availability < 0.999 or failover time > acceptable threshold. Ownership is unambiguous.

recommended_revisions: Establish detailed Network Link interface specification (carrier metrics, failover triggers, SLA).
Define SafetyAlert interface contract (alert types, latency, acknowledgment protocol).
Establish acceptance criteria for failover time (e.g., <5 seconds) and link recovery time.
Conduct design review with Vehicle and Safety Services modules to confirm interface contracts.
Plan for failure mode testing (carrier outages, latency spikes, packet loss) during development.

decision.justification: FR-NGHE-010 is not atomic; it decomposes into four sub-FRs (command link, telemetry link, failure detection/recovery, event logging). Each sub-FR requires further refinement and DP selection. The refined FR and selected DP are sound, and the Teleoperation module assignment is justified. However, the sub-FRs must be decomposed to the next level to ensure completeness and traceability. Proceeding to the next decomposition level is necessary to validate the DP and identify any hidden couplings or risks.
next_level_sub_frs: FR-NGHE-010.1: The teleoperation system shall establish and maintain a bidirectional command link from tele-operator to vehicle with latency ≤ [customer-defined threshold, e.g., 500 ms].
FR-NGHE-010.2: The teleoperation system shall establish and maintain a bidirectional telemetry link from vehicle to tele-operator with latency ≤ [customer-defined threshold, e.g., 500 ms].
FR-NGHE-010.3: The teleoperation system shall detect link failures (command or telemetry) and initiate recovery within [customer-defined time, e.g., 5 seconds].
FR-NGHE-010.4: The teleoperation system shall log link state events (up/down transitions, carrier switches, recovery attempts) with timestamps for post-evaluation availability calculation.

assumptions: Multi-carrier network services (LTE + satellite or equivalent) are available at the worksite.
Vehicle can accommodate dual network hardware (antenna, modem, power budget).
Tele-operator is trained on failover procedures and can tolerate brief link interruptions during carrier switchover.
Network Link interface will expose carrier-level metrics (latency, packet loss, signal strength) for intelligent failover decisions.
SafetyAlert interface will support asynchronous event notification without blocking Teleoperation module.
Evaluation period is customer-defined and sufficient to capture representative network conditions (e.g., 30 days).
open_risks: Risk: Satellite latency may exceed operator tolerance; mitigation: design for LTE primary with satellite as backup, and conduct human-factors testing.
Risk: Failover logic complexity increases software defect probability; mitigation: rigorous testing, formal verification of state machine, and code review.
Risk: Cost of dual-carrier hardware and service subscriptions may exceed budget; mitigation: early cost-benefit analysis with customer.
Risk: Network Link interface may not expose sufficient metrics for intelligent failover; mitigation: establish detailed interface specification early.
Risk: Vehicle-side network hardware faults may degrade link quality; mitigation: include diagnostics and escalation to Vehicle module.
Risk: SafetyAlert notification may introduce latency or coupling; mitigation: design as fire-and-forget event stream.
evidence_needed: Customer-defined link availability target (confirm 0.999 or alternative).
Worksite network coverage map (LTE + satellite availability).
Vehicle power budget and antenna design constraints.
Operator latency tolerance and failover procedure acceptance criteria.
Network Link interface specification (carrier metrics, SLA, failover triggers).
SafetyAlert interface specification (alert types, latency, acknowledgment).
Failure mode testing plan (carrier outages, latency spikes, packet loss).
Cost-benefit analysis for dual-carrier hardware and service subscriptions.
